nodes.name: Duty Cycle TIG 240V
nodes.summary: TIG duty cycle at 240VAC/60Hz input: 30% @ 175A, 100% @ 105A.

nodes.name: Welding Current Range TIG 120V
nodes.summary: TIG welding current range at 120VAC input: 10A–125A.

nodes.name: Welding Current Range TIG 240V
nodes.summary: TIG welding current range at 240VAC input: 10A–175A.

nodes.name: Current Input at Output TIG 120V
nodes.summary: TIG current input at 120VAC: 20.6A at 125A output.
aliases: how many amps it pulls at 120V TIG

nodes.name: Current Input at Output TIG 240V
nodes.summary: TIG current input at 240VAC: 15.6A at 175A output.
aliases: how many amps it pulls at 240V TIG

nodes.name: Maximum OCV TIG
nodes.summary: Maximum open-circuit voltage for TIG process: 86VDC, applies to both 120V and 240V input.

nodes.name: Duty Cycle Stick 120V
nodes.summary: Stick duty cycle at 120VAC/60Hz input: 40% @ 80A, 100% @ 60A.

nodes.name: Duty Cycle Stick 240V
nodes.summary: Stick duty cycle at 240VAC/60Hz input: 25% @ 175A, 100% @ 100A.

nodes.name: Welding Current Range Stick 120V
nodes.summary: Stick welding current range at 120VAC input: 10A–80A.

nodes.name: Welding Current Range Stick 240V
nodes.summary: Stick welding current range at 240VAC input: 10A–175A.

nodes.name: Current Input at Output Stick 120V
nodes.summary: Stick current input at 120VAC: 19.5A at 80A output.
aliases: how many amps it pulls on regular outlet

nodes.name: Current Input at Output Stick 240V
nodes.summary: Stick current input at 240VAC: 23.7A at 175A output.

nodes.name: Maximum OCV Stick
nodes.summary: Maximum open-circuit voltage for Stick process: 86VDC, applies to both 120V and 240V input.

nodes.name: Weldable Materials MIG
nodes.summary: MIG can weld Mild Steel and Stainless Steel standard; Aluminum requires optional Spool Gun.
aliases: what can I weld with MIG

nodes.name: Weldable Materials TIG
nodes.summary: TIG can weld Mild Steel, Stainless Steel, and Chrome Moly.
aliases: what can I weld with TIG

nodes.name: Weldable Materials Stick
nodes.summary: Stick can weld Mild Steel and Stainless Steel.
aliases: what can I weld with the stinger

nodes.name: Spool Gun
nodes.summary: Optional accessory required to MIG-weld aluminum on this machine.

nodes.name: Home Button
nodes.summary: Front panel button used to navigate the LCD Display, located at the top corner of the screen.

nodes.name: Back Button
nodes.summary: Front panel button used to navigate the LCD Display, located at the top corner of the screen opposite the Home Button.

nodes.name: Control Knob
nodes.summary: Center push-turn dial on the front panel used for adjusting machine settings.

nodes.name: Left Knob
nodes.summary: Dial flanking the left side of the Control Knob used to adjust welding parameters.

nodes.name: Right Knob
nodes.summary: Dial flanking the right side of the Control Knob used to adjust welding parameters.

nodes.name: LCD Display
nodes.summary: Screen labeled OMNIPRO 220 that shows welding parameters and menu navigation.

nodes.name: Power Switch
nodes.summary: I/O rocker switch on the front panel used to turn the machine on and off.

nodes.name: Storage Compartment
nodes.summary: Storage area behind the Vulcan logo grille on the front panel.

nodes.name: MIG Gun/Spool Gun Cable Socket
nodes.summary: Round socket on the left side of the lower front panel for connecting a MIG gun or spool gun cable.

nodes.name: Spool Gun Gas Outlet
nodes.summary: Leftmost port on the bottom row of the front panel; connects the optional Spool Gun's gas hose.

nodes.name: Negative Socket
nodes.summary: Socket marked with a minus sign on the bottom row of the front panel for negative polarity cable connection.

nodes.name: Positive Socket
nodes.summary: Socket marked with a plus sign, rightmost port on the bottom row of the front panel, for positive polarity cable connection.

nodes.name: Wire Feed Power Cable Port
nodes.summary: Small round port between the negative and positive sockets on the bottom row of the front panel, used for the wire feed power cable.
aliases: small round port in the middle

nodes.name: Connect Spool Gun Gas Hose
nodes.summary: When using an optional Spool Gun (sold separately), connect the Spool Gun gas hose to the Spool Gun Gas Outlet on the front panel.
aliases: connecting the spool gun gas line
hooking up spool gun gas hose

nodes.name: Cold Wire Feed Switch
nodes.summary: Small switch near the top center of the interior compartment allowing wire to be fed without energizing the circuit.

nodes.name: Feed Tensioner
nodes.summary: Arm that presses on the feed rollers to maintain correct wire feed tension inside the wire feed mechanism.

nodes.name: Idler Arm
nodes.summary: Pivoting arm on the wire feed mechanism that helps guide and tension the welding wire.

nodes.name: Wire Feed Mechanism
nodes.summary: Assembly labeled 'VULCAN' housing the feed rollers, tensioner, idler arm, and feed roller knob that drives welding wire through the gun.

nodes.name: Wire Spool
nodes.summary: Spool of welding wire mounted on the left side of the interior compartment, secured by the Spool Knob.

nodes.name: Spool Knob
nodes.summary: Knob that secures the Wire Spool in place inside the interior compartment.
aliases: the knob that holds the spool

nodes.name: Wire Inlet Liner
nodes.summary: Tube that guides welding wire from the spool into the wire feed mechanism.

nodes.name: Feed Roller Knob
nodes.summary: Knob used to adjust the feed rollers within the wire feed mechanism.

nodes.name: Foot Pedal Socket
nodes.summary: Round multi-pin socket at the bottom left of the interior panel for connecting an optional foot pedal.

nodes.name: Wire Feed Control Socket
nodes.summary: Round multi-pin socket at the bottom right of the interior panel used for wire feed control connections.

nodes.name: 1-2 lb Wire Spool Installation
nodes.summary: Steps to open the welder door, remove wingnut/spacer, place a 1-2 lb wire spool on the spindle against the brake pad so it unwinds clockwise, then secure with spacer and wingnut.

nodes.name: 10-12 lb Wire Spool Installation
nodes.summary: Steps to install a 10-12 lb wire spool using the Spool Adapter: remove wingnut/spacer, place adapter and spool (aligned to unwind clockwise), secure with spacer/wingnut, screw in spool knob, then release feed tensioner and idler arm.

nodes.name: Feed Roller Selection and Replacement
nodes.summary: Verify feed roller matches wire type (solid core V-groove or flux-cored knurled groove) and diameter marked on spool; unscrew knob, flip/replace roller, reinstall knob.

nodes.name: Gun Cable Connector Installation
nodes.summary: Loosen wire feed knob, insert gun cable connector fully into wire feed socket, tighten knob without overtightening; incomplete insertion causes gas leak at connection.

nodes.name: Wire Feed Control Cable Connection
nodes.summary: Insert Wire Feed Control Cable through hole on welder front into the Wire Feed Control Socket (single correct orientation) and tighten the lock ring.
aliases: hooking up the feed control cable

nodes.name: DCEN Polarity Setup for Flux-Cored Welding
nodes.summary: For gasless flux-cored welding, plug Ground Clamp Cable into Positive socket and Wire Feed Power Cable into Negative socket; twist cables clockwise to lock.

nodes.name: DCEP Polarity Setup for Solid Core Welding
nodes.summary: For gas-shielded solid core welding, plug Ground Clamp Cable into Negative socket and Wire Feed Power Cable into Positive socket; twist clockwise to lock, then set up shielding gas cylinder, regulator, and hose.

nodes.name: Gas Cylinder and Regulator Setup
nodes.summary: Secure gas cylinder with straps, purge valve, thread regulator onto cylinder (using CGA 580/320 adapter for C100 gas), and attach gas hose to welder inlet, wrench-tightening all connections.

nodes.name: Door Latch
nodes.summary: Latch that must be pulled up to open the welder door for wire compartment access.

nodes.name: Welder Door
nodes.summary: Door panel providing access to the wire spool compartment, opened via the door latch.

nodes.name: Wingnut
nodes.summary: Secures the wire spool (or spool adapter) onto the spool spindle; if loose, spool spins freely causing feed problems.

nodes.name: Spacer
nodes.summary: Cylindrical spacer placed over the spool spindle before securing spool with wingnut.

nodes.name: Spool Spindle
nodes.summary: Spindle onto which the wire spool (or spool adapter) is mounted against the spool brake pad.

nodes.name: Spool Brake Pad
nodes.summary: Pad against which the wire spool or spool adapter is seated on the spindle to provide braking friction.

nodes.name: Spool Adapter
nodes.summary: Adapter placed over the spool spindle for 10-12 lb spools; aligns via pin/hole with the spool and accepts the spool knob.

nodes.name: Spool Knob
nodes.summary: Knurled knob that screws into the spool adapter to secure a 10-12 lb wire spool assembly.

nodes.name: Feed Roller
nodes.summary: Roller inside the wire feed housing; must match wire type (solid core V-groove or flux-cored knurled groove) and wire diameter.

nodes.name: Feed Roller Knob
nodes.summary: Knob unscrewed counterclockwise to expose and allow flipping/replacing the feed roller, then reinstalled to secure it.

nodes.name: Solid Core V-Groove Roller Sizes
nodes.summary: V-Groove feed roller for solid core wire with grooves for 0.030/0.035 and 0.025 wire diameters.

nodes.name: Flux-Cored Knurled Groove Roller Sizes
nodes.summary: Knurled Groove feed roller for flux-cored wire with grooves for 0.045 and 0.030/0.035 wire diameters.

nodes.name: Gun Cable Connector
nodes.summary: Connector inserted through the welder front into the Wire Feed socket; must be fully inserted to prevent gas leaks.

nodes.name: Wire Feed Control Cable
nodes.summary: Cable connecting to the Wire Feed Control Socket in one specific orientation; secured via a lock ring.

nodes.name: Ground Clamp Cable
nodes.summary: Cable connecting the ground clamp; plugs into Positive socket for DCEN flux-cored or Negative socket for DCEP solid core.

nodes.name: Wire Feed Power Cable
nodes.summary: Cable powering the wire feed; plugs into Negative socket for DCEN flux-cored or Positive socket for DCEP solid core.

nodes.name: Gas Regulator
nodes.summary: Included regulator threaded onto the gas cylinder; may require CGA 580/320 adapter for C100 gas; connects to gas hose outlet.

nodes.name: Gas Hose
nodes.summary: Included hose connecting regulator outlet to welder's gas inlet, wrench-tightened at both ends.

nodes.name: CGA 580/320 Adapter
nodes.summary: Enclosed adapter required when using C100 shielding gas, connected between regulator inlet and gas cylinder.

nodes.name: Power Off and Unplug Before Setup
nodes.summary: Warning to turn Power Switch off and unplug the welder before setup or wire spool installation to prevent serious injury from accidental operation.

nodes.name: Do Not Overtighten Wire Feed Knob
nodes.summary: Notice warning not to overtighten the wire feed mechanism knob to prevent damage.

nodes.name: Secure Gas Cylinder to Prevent Tipping
nodes.summary: Gas cylinder must be secured with two straps on a cabinet or cart to prevent tipping.
aliases: don't let the tank tip over

nodes.name: Wire Spool Spins Freely (Loose Wingnut)
nodes.summary: If the wire spool can spin freely on the spindle, the wingnut is too loose, causing wire to unravel, unspool, tangle, and create feeding problems.

nodes.name: Gas Connection Leak from Incomplete Gun Cable Insertion
nodes.summary: If the Gun Cable Connector is not fully inserted into the Wire Feed socket, the gas connection leaks, preventing shielding gas from reaching the welding arc.
aliases: gun not seated all the way

nodes.name: Mismatched Feed Roller / Wire Diameter
nodes.summary: Using a feed roller groove or type that doesn't match the wire diameter or wire type (solid core vs flux-cored) marked on the spool causes feed issues.

nodes.name: Rating Plate Vulcan OMNIPRO 220
nodes.summary: Rating plate data: Item 57812, IP21S, conforms to ANSI/IEC 60974-1, U0=86V no-load voltage, includes duty cycle tables for 240V and 120V input across multiple processes.

nodes.name: Wire Inlet Liner
nodes.summary: Liner that guides welding wire from the spool into the feed guide and gun; wire must be fed at least 12 inches into it during loading.

nodes.name: Feed Guide
nodes.summary: Guide on the wire feed mechanism housing that receives wire from the Wire Inlet Liner.

nodes.name: Feed Roller
nodes.summary: Roller with a groove that the welding wire rests in as it is driven through the feed mechanism.

nodes.name: MIG Gun Nozzle
nodes.summary: Removable nozzle tip on the MIG Gun; pulled off to remove, replaced after contact tip installation.

nodes.name: Contact Tip
nodes.summary: Threaded tip behind the nozzle that must match the wire type; removed by unscrewing counterclockwise, installed by threading clockwise and tightening.

nodes.name: MIG Gun
nodes.summary: Gun assembly with cable, nozzle, contact tip, and trigger used to feed wire and strike the welding arc.

nodes.name: Load MIG Welding Wire
nodes.summary: Steps 18-23: trim wire end, feed at least 12 inches through Wire Inlet Liner and Feed Guide, seat wire in Feed Roller groove, latch Feed Tensioner over Idler Arm, remove Nozzle and Contact Tip, and lay gun cable straight.

nodes.name: Feed Tensioner Setting - Solid Wire
nodes.summary: Recommended tensioner setting of 3-5 for solid MIG wire.

nodes.name: Feed Tensioner Setting - Flux-Cored Wire
nodes.summary: Recommended tensioner setting of 2-3 for flux-cored wire; excess tension crushes the wire and can cause feeding issues.

nodes.name: Flux-Cored Wire Crushed by Excess Tensioner Force
nodes.summary: Too much Feed Tensioner force on flux-cored wire crushes it, causing feeding problems.

nodes.name: Wire Unspooling and Tangling During Loading
nodes.summary: If tension is not kept on the wire end while loading, the wire unravels and unspools, causing tangling and feeding problems.

nodes.name: Stainless Steel Wire Feeding Difficulty
nodes.summary: Stainless steel wire is less flexible than other wire types and is more difficult to feed through the liner and gun, especially if the gun cable is not kept straight.

nodes.name: Lay MIG Gun Cable Straight
nodes.summary: Lay the MIG Gun cable in a straight line so wire moves through it easily, leaving the cover open to observe the feed mechanism; especially important for stainless steel wire.

nodes.name: Power Input Socket
nodes.summary: Keyed socket accepting either 120VAC or 240VAC power cord plug; plug fits only one way.

nodes.name: Power Up and Cold-Feed Wire
nodes.summary: Steps 24-26: plug power cord into Power Input Socket, connect to grounded/GFCI receptacle and turn on Power Switch, then use Cold Wire Feed Switch to feed wire through the gun about two inches.
aliases: plugging in and priming the wire

nodes.name: Welding Voltage Shock Hazard
nodes.summary: DANGER: parts may be at welding voltage; keep hands away from wire feed mechanism, close door before plugging in (unless using Cold Wire Feed), and do not touch trigger while feeding wire, to prevent electric shock and death.

nodes.name: Wire Not Feeding / Spool Stationary
nodes.summary: If wire does not feed properly and the spool is stationary, turn off and unplug the welder and slightly tighten the Feed Tensioner clockwise before retrying.
aliases: wire won't feed and spool not turning

nodes.name: Check Wire Drive Tension
nodes.summary: Step 27: press and hold trigger to feed wire against wood 2-3 inches away; wire should bend (proper tension) rather than stop feeding within 3 seconds (no-arc timeout).

nodes.name: Wire Stops Instead of Bending During Tension Check
nodes.summary: If the wire stops feeding instead of bending against the test wood, tension is insufficient; unplug welder, slightly tighten Feed Tensioner clockwise, and retest.

nodes.name: Assemble Contact Tip and Nozzle
nodes.summary: Steps 28-31: turn off and unplug welder, close and latch door, select a Contact Tip compatible with the wire, thread it clockwise into the gun and tighten, replace Nozzle, and cut wire to 1/2 inch stickout.

nodes.name: Optional Spool Gun Setup
nodes.summary: Steps 1-6: plug Ground Clamp Cable into Negative Socket and Wire Feed Power Cable into Positive Socket (twist clockwise to lock), insert Spool Gun Cable Connector into Wire Feed socket and tighten knob, connect Wire Feed Control Cable, connect Gas Hose to Spool Gun Gas Outlet, and refer to Spool Gun manual for operation.

nodes.name: Negative Socket
nodes.summary: Negative (-) output socket; Ground Clamp Cable plugs in here for Spool Gun setup, twisted clockwise to lock.

nodes.name: Positive Socket
nodes.summary: Positive (+) output socket; Wire Feed Power Cable plugs in here for Spool Gun setup, twisted clockwise to lock.

nodes.name: Spool Gun Cable Connector Not Fully Inserted
nodes.summary: If the Spool Gun Cable Connector is not fully inserted, the gas connection will leak, preventing shielding gas from reaching the welding arc.
aliases: gas leak from spool gun plug

nodes.name: Do Not Overtighten Spool Gun Knob
nodes.summary: NOTICE: To prevent damage, do not overtighten the Knob securing the Spool Gun Cable Connector.
aliases: easy on the spool gun knob

nodes.name: Aluminum Wire Welding
nodes.summary: Aluminum welding can be performed only with an optional Spool Gun using aluminum wire and shielding gas.

nodes.name: Protective Gear Warning
nodes.summary: WARNING to prevent serious injury: wear minimum shade 10 full face shield or welding mask, ear protection, welding gloves, sleeves and apron, NIOSH-approved respirator, and fire resistant work clothes without pockets; arc light can cause permanent eye/skin damage; do not breathe arc fumes.

nodes.name: Keep Welding Tip Clear of Grounded Objects
nodes.summary: WARNING to prevent serious injury, fire, and burns: keep welding tip clear of grounded objects whenever unit is plugged in and turned on.
aliases: keep tip away from metal warning

nodes.name: Practice Welds on Scrap
nodes.summary: Recommended practice welding on scrap material before working on an actual project, and additional practice whenever material thickness, material type, connection type, or process changes.

nodes.name: Output Rating 240V Process A
nodes.summary: 240V input output rating table: 200A/24V at 25% duty, 130A/20.5V at 60% duty, 115A/19.75V at 100% duty; U0=86V, I1max up to 25.5A, I1eff up to 12.8A.

nodes.name: Output Rating 240V Process B
nodes.summary: 240V input output rating table: 175A/27V at 25% duty, 115A/24.6V at 60% duty, 100A/24V at 100% duty; U0=86V.

nodes.name: Output Rating 240V Process C
nodes.summary: 240V input output rating table: 175A/17V at 30% duty, 125A/15V at 60% duty, 105A/14.2V at 100% duty; U0=86V.

nodes.name: Output Rating 120V Process A
nodes.summary: 120V input output rating table: 140A/21V at 40% duty, 85A/18.25V at 60% duty, 75A/17.75V at 100% duty; U0=86V, I1max up to 20.8A, I1eff up to 13.1A.

nodes.name: Output Rating 120V Process B
nodes.summary: 120V input output rating table: 80A/23.2V at 40% duty, 70A/22.8V at 60% duty, 60A/22.4V at 100% duty; U0=86V.

nodes.name: Output Rating 120V Process C
nodes.summary: 120V input output rating table: 125A/15V at 40% duty, 105A/14.2V at 60% duty, 90A/13.6V at 100% duty; U0=86V.
aliases: how long I can weld on house power
the amp/volt table for regular outlets

nodes.name: Welder Overheat / Thermal Shutdown
nodes.summary: Exceeding duty cycle causes the internal thermal protection system to shut down the welder automatically, showing a warning on the LCD Display; it resumes automatically after cooling.
aliases: machine getting too hot and stopping

nodes.name: Cool Down After Thermal Shutdown
nodes.summary: Rest the MIG Gun on an electrically non-conductive, heat-proof surface (e.g., concrete slab) well clear of the ground clamp, leave Power Switch ON so the fan cools the unit, then use shorter welding periods and longer rest periods when resuming.

nodes.name: Setting Up The Weld
nodes.summary: Clean weld surfaces to bare metal, clamp workpieces with correct gap and chamfer thick edges as needed, disconnect vehicle battery when welding on a vehicle, and clamp ground cable to bare metal near the weld area.
aliases: getting the joint ready to weld

nodes.name: Disconnect Vehicle Battery Before Welding on Vehicle
nodes.summary: NOTICE: When welding equipment on a vehicle, disconnect the vehicle battery power from both positive and ground connections before welding to prevent damage to vehicle electronics from high voltage/high frequency bursts.
aliases: pull the battery cables before welding

nodes.name: Ground Clamp
nodes.summary: Clamp connected to bare metal on the workpiece (or metal workbench) near the weld area; ground connection polarity depends on desired welding polarity.

nodes.name: Flux-Cored Wire Welding
nodes.summary: Welds mild steel and stainless steel without shielding gas; uses lower Feed Tensioner setting (2-3) than solid wire due to wire crush risk.

nodes.name: MIG Welding (Solid Wire)
nodes.summary: Uses solid wire and shielding gas to weld mild steel and stainless steel; can weld thinner workpieces than flux-cored welding.

nodes.name: Power-Up Sequence
nodes.summary: Steps 4-6: plug power cord into properly grounded, GFCI protected 120VAC (20A) or 240VAC receptacle with delayed-action circuit breaker/fuses, set MIG Gun on nonconductive surface, turn Power Switch ON.
aliases: plugging it in and powering up

nodes.name: Control Panel
nodes.summary: Panel with Home Button, Back Button, Main Control Knob, Left Knob, and Right Knob used to navigate the Settings menu and LCD display.

nodes.name: Main Control Knob
nodes.summary: Knob used to select process, navigate menu screens, and adjust Optional Settings.

nodes.name: Left Knob
nodes.summary: Knob that sets wire diameter and adjusts Wire Feed Speed/Amperage in Auto Weld Settings.

nodes.name: Right Knob
nodes.summary: Knob that sets material thickness and adjusts Voltage in Auto Weld Settings.

nodes.name: Settings Menu Navigation
nodes.summary: Press Home Button, turn Main Control Knob to select process, press to confirm; navigate polarity/gas, wire diameter/material thickness, and auto weld settings screens.
aliases: using the home button to set stuff

nodes.name: Polarity and Gas Settings
nodes.summary: Screen for plugging cables per polarity and connecting gas; set SCFH between 20-30 (e.g., Stainless Tri-Mix example).
aliases: setting up polarity and gas flow

nodes.name: Wire Diameter and Material Thickness Setting
nodes.summary: Left Knob sets wire diameter, Right Knob sets material thickness; example shows MIG Stainless Steel, .025in diameter, 24Ga thickness.
aliases: dialing in wire gauge and plate thickness
wire size and metal thickness setting

nodes.name: Auto Weld Settings
nodes.summary: Left Knob adjusts Wire Feed Speed(Amperage), Right Knob adjusts Voltage; white mark on display indicates recommended setting for selected wire/electrode diameter and workpiece thickness. Example: 121 in/min, 0A, 13.8V, MIG Steel, C25 gas, Run-In WFS 25%, Inductance 5, Wire Diameter .030in, Thickness 24Ga, Spot Timer 0.0S.

nodes.name: Run-In WFS
nodes.summary: Adjusts wire speed before it contacts the workpiece, expressed as a % of the pre-set wire feed speed (WFS).

nodes.name: Inductance
nodes.summary: Adjusts arc length; increase for a more fluid puddle and flatter bead, decrease for a colder puddle.

nodes.name: Spot Timer
nodes.summary: Sets timer duration for spot welding.

nodes.name: Recall Setting
nodes.summary: Retrieve previously saved welder settings from one of 5 memory slots.

nodes.name: Save Setting
nodes.summary: Program up to 5 configurations: scroll to a slot with Main Control Knob, press to access OVERWRITE MEMORY SETTING screen, select YES, press to save current settings.

nodes.name: Shielding Gas Asphyxiation Danger
nodes.summary: DANGER: Shielding gas can displace air causing rapid loss of consciousness and death; do not open gas without proper ventilation, fix leaks immediately. Gas without CO2 can be more hazardous since asphyxiation can start without shortness of breath.

nodes.name: Gas Cylinder Setup (Gas Shielded Solid-Core Wire)
nodes.summary: Open gas cylinder valve all the way; set Flow Gauge to SCFH value indicated on Settings Chart.

nodes.name: Basic Wire Welding Technique
nodes.summary: Press and hold trigger to ignite arc; use stringer bead for narrow welds or weave bead for wider welds; direct wire straight into joint.

nodes.name: Gun Angle for Butt and Fillet Welds
nodes.summary: 90° gun angle (straight up/down) for butt welds; 45° gun angle for fillet (T-shaped) welds.
aliases: how to tilt the gun for T-joints

nodes.name: Push Angle (Solid Wire with Shielding Gas)
nodes.summary: Gun tilted 0-15° away from the welding direction for solid wire with shielding gas (MIG).

nodes.name: Drag Angle (Flux-Cored Wire without Gas)
nodes.summary: Gun tilted 0-15° in the direction of welding for flux-cored wire without shielding gas.

nodes.name: Contact Tip to Work Distance (CTWD)
nodes.summary: Contact Tip should remain within 1/2 inch of the work surface (CTWD).
aliases: how far the tip should be from metal

nodes.name: Thermal Shutdown (Duty Cycle Exceeded)
nodes.summary: If welder is used too long, warning screen appears and unit automatically shuts down; auto-resumes after cooling. Keep Power Switch ON so internal fan can cool the unit. No circuit breaker to reset.

nodes.name: Low Voltage Input Fault
nodes.summary: Warning screen fault indicating input voltage below specified limit; inspect input voltage and ensure within machine limits.

nodes.name: High Voltage Input Fault
nodes.summary: Warning screen fault indicating input voltage higher than specified limit; inspect input voltage and ensure within machine limits.

nodes.name: Thermal Cool-Down Procedure
nodes.summary: During thermal shutdown, rest MIG Gun on non-conductive heat-proof surface away from ground clamp, keep Power Switch ON to allow fan cooling; use shorter welding periods and longer rests when resuming.

nodes.name: Hot Weld Caution
nodes.summary: CAUTION: weld will be hot after welding, do not touch.

nodes.name: Shutdown and Storage Procedure
nodes.summary: Steps 8-12: set MIG Gun on heat-proof surface, turn Power Switch OFF, cool down, unplug power cord, remove ground clamp, disconnect MIG gun, respool wire, and for MIG close/secure gas cylinder valve and remove regulator.
aliases: shutting down and putting it away
turning off and storing the welder

nodes.name: Respool Wire Procedure
nodes.summary: Clip wire, remove gas nozzle/contact tip, release Idler Arm on Wire Feed mechanism, rotate Wire Spool counterclockwise while holding wire securely to prevent unraveling.

nodes.name: Gas Cylinder Shutdown (MIG Only)
nodes.summary: Close shielding gas cylinder valve securely, remove regulator and replace cap, disconnect gas hose from welder, store and secure gas cylinder.

nodes.name: TIG Connect Cables
nodes.summary: 4-step procedure to connect Ground Clamp Cable to Positive Socket, TIG Torch Cable to Negative Socket, Foot Pedal Cable to Foot Pedal Socket, and Shielding Gas Hose for TIG welding setup.
aliases: connecting the TIG torch and ground

nodes.name: Turn Off and Unplug Before Setup
nodes.summary: Warning to prevent serious injury from accidental operation: turn Power Switch off and unplug Welder before setup.
aliases: don't set up while it's on
turn it off before setup warning

nodes.name: Foot Pedal
nodes.summary: Sold-separately accessory that connects via cable through a front hole to the Foot Pedal Socket inside the welder for TIG amperage control.

nodes.name: TIG Torch
nodes.summary: Sold-separately torch used for TIG welding; plugs into Negative Socket; assembled from Collet, Collet Body, Ceramic Nozzle, Back Cap, and Tungsten Electrode.

nodes.name: Ground Clamp
nodes.summary: Clamp at end of Ground Clamp Cable; plugs into Positive Socket for TIG welding and Negative Socket for Stick welding.

nodes.name: Electrode Holder
nodes.summary: Stick welding tool that holds the Stick Electrode; cable plugs into Positive Socket.

nodes.name: Connect Shielding Gas
nodes.summary: 4-step procedure to secure an Argon cylinder, purge the valve, thread on the included Regulator, and connect the Shielding Gas Hose from the TIG Torch Cable Connector to the Regulator Outlet.

nodes.name: 100% Argon Cylinder
nodes.summary: Shielding gas cylinder (not included) required for TIG welding; must be secured with two straps to prevent tipping.

nodes.name: Gas Regulator
nodes.summary: Included regulator threaded onto the Argon cylinder; its Outlet connects to the Shielding Gas Hose.

nodes.name: Shielding Gas Hose
nodes.summary: Hose connecting the TIG Torch Cable Connector to the Regulator Outlet to supply Argon shielding gas.

nodes.name: Power Input Socket
nodes.summary: Front panel socket accepting either 120VAC or 240VAC power cord; plug only fits one way.

nodes.name: Connect Power Cord
nodes.summary: Plug either 120VAC or 240VAC Power Cord into the Power Input Socket; plug only fits one way; do not plug into wall outlet during setup.
aliases: connecting the cord to the machine

nodes.name: Do Not Plug Into Wall Outlet During Setup
nodes.summary: Warning to prevent serious injury from accidental operation by not plugging the power cord into the wall outlet during setup.
aliases: don't plug into the wall yet warning
keep it unplugged during setup notice

nodes.name: Stick Connect Cables
nodes.summary: 2-step procedure: plug Ground Clamp Cable into Negative Socket and Electrode Holder Cable into Positive Socket, twisting clockwise to lock.
aliases: plugging in the stinger and ground

nodes.name: Input Current Ratings I1max/I1eff
nodes.summary: Rated input current values (I1max and I1eff) for both 240V and 120V input voltages across three current ranges; also lists open circuit voltage U0=86V and IP21S rating.

nodes.name: Tungsten Electrode Contamination
nodes.summary: Electrode becomes contaminated or dulled, degrading weld quality; caused by using a shared grinding wheel or pulling electrode from the rear of the torch.

nodes.name: Sharpen Tungsten Electrode
nodes.summary: 6-step procedure to safely remove, inspect, and grind a Tungsten Electrode to a blunt conical point using a dedicated fine grit grinding wheel, then reinsert with proper protrusion.

nodes.name: Hazardous Materials in Electrodes Warning
nodes.summary: Warning that some electrodes contain materials hazardous to breathe; wear a respirator and ANSI-approved safety goggles when grinding.

nodes.name: Assemble TIG Torch
nodes.summary: 8-step procedure to select correct Tungsten Electrode size per Settings Chart, match Collet and Collet Body, thread Collet Body and Ceramic Nozzle, insert Collet and Electrode, and lock with Back Cap.
aliases: loading the tungsten into the torch

nodes.name: Collet
nodes.summary: TIG torch component that must be matched in size to the Tungsten Electrode; inserted into back of torch and into Collet Body.

nodes.name: Collet Body
nodes.summary: TIG torch component threaded into the front of the torch; sized to match Tungsten Electrode and Collet; Ceramic Nozzle threads onto it.

nodes.name: Ceramic Nozzle
nodes.summary: TIG torch nozzle threaded onto Collet Body; size must be appropriate for application; Electrode should protrude 1/8-1/4 inch beyond it.

nodes.name: Back Cap
nodes.summary: TIG torch component that locks the Tungsten Electrode in place after insertion into the Collet.

nodes.name: Tungsten Electrode
nodes.summary: Sold-separately TIG welding electrode; size selected per material thickness using Settings Chart; ground to a blunt conical tip and protrudes 1/8-1/4 inch beyond Ceramic Nozzle.

nodes.name: Tungsten Electrode Size Selection
nodes.summary: Electrode size chosen based on material thickness, per the Settings Chart located on top of the Welder.

nodes.name: TIG/Stick Protective Gear Warning
nodes.summary: Warning requiring minimum shade 10 full face shield, ear protection, welding gloves, sleeves and apron, NIOSH-approved respirator, and fire resistant clothing without pockets; arc light can damage eyes and skin; do not breathe arc fumes.

nodes.name: Keep Welding Tip Clear of Grounded Objects
nodes.summary: Warning to prevent serious injury, fire and burns by keeping the welding tip clear of grounded objects whenever the unit is plugged in and turned on.
aliases: don't touch tip to metal warning
keep torch tip away from ground stuff

nodes.name: DC TIG Welding
nodes.summary: Used to weld mild steel and stainless steel using a TIG Rod and shielding gas.

nodes.name: AC TIG Welding
nodes.summary: Used to weld aluminum using a TIG Rod and shielding gas; requires AC output.

nodes.name: Welder Overheat Shutdown
nodes.summary: When welder exceeds duty cycle limits and overheats, internal thermal protection automatically shuts down the unit and shows a warning on the LCD display; it resumes automatically after cooling.

nodes.name: Thermal Protection Cooldown Procedure
nodes.summary: Rest torch/electrode holder on nonconductive heat-proof surface away from ground clamp, leave Power Switch ON so internal fan cools welder, then use shorter welding/longer resting periods when resuming.

nodes.name: Setting Up the Weld
nodes.summary: Practice on scrap of same thickness, clean weld surfaces to bare metal, clamp workpieces with controlled gap, chamfer thick edges, and disconnect vehicle battery before welding on vehicles.

nodes.name: Ground Cable Connection
nodes.summary: Clamp Ground Cable to bare metal on the workpiece near the weld area, or to a metal work bench where the workpiece is clamped; ground connection point depends on desired welding polarity.

nodes.name: TIG Grounding and Torch Handling Warning
nodes.summary: Do not weld without Grounding Clamp; TIG Torch must rest on a nonconductive, nonflammable surface when not held; only hold TIG Rod with an electrically insulated welding glove.
aliases: don't weld without ground clamp warning

nodes.name: TIG Setup and Startup Procedure
nodes.summary: Steps 1-9: open gas cylinder valve, set flow gauge per settings chart, plug into grounded GFCI-protected 120V/240V outlet, set torch down, power on, navigate control panel Home/Main Knob to select TIG process, then adjust polarity/gas, rod diameter/material thickness, and auto weld amperage settings.

nodes.name: TIG Polarity and Gas Settings
nodes.summary: Plug cables and connect gas according to screen; set SCFH gas flow between 10-25; example shown 100% Argon.

nodes.name: TIG Rod Diameter and Material Thickness Setting
nodes.summary: Turn Left Knob to set rod diameter, Right Knob to set material thickness; example shown 1/16" rod diameter and 24 Ga material thickness.

nodes.name: TIG Auto Weld Amperage Setting
nodes.summary: Turn Left Knob to adjust output amperage; turn Right Knob to ON to energize TIG Torch, creating Open Circuit Voltage. Example shown: 30A output.

nodes.name: TIG Open Circuit Voltage Shock Hazard
nodes.summary: When Right Knob is turned to ON to energize TIG Torch, Open Circuit Voltage is present, presenting a serious injury/shock hazard.
aliases: getting zapped when torch is hot

nodes.name: TIG Control Panel Knobs
nodes.summary: Control panel with Home Button, Back Button, Left Knob, Main Control Knob, and Right Knob used to navigate and adjust TIG process settings.

nodes.name: TIG Optional Settings Menu
nodes.summary: Optional settings menu accessed via Main Control Knob offering Recall Setting (retrieve saved settings) and Save Setting (program up to 5 configurations).

nodes.name: Required Protective Gear for Welding
nodes.summary: Minimum shade number 10 full face shield or welding mask, ear protection, welding gloves, sleeves and apron, NIOSH-approved respirator, and fire resistant work clothes without pockets required; arc light can cause permanent eye/skin damage; do not breathe arc fumes.

nodes.name: TIG Metal Work Bench Must Be Grounded
nodes.summary: Metal work bench must be grounded when TIG welding to prevent serious injury.

nodes.name: TIG Welding Technique
nodes.summary: Steps 10-15: hold torch and rod in gloved hands, maintain electrode-to-workpiece distance of 1-1.5x electrode diameter, open torch gas valve, initiate arc via foot pedal or touch-and-lift, tilt torch 10-15 degrees and add rod material to puddle, remove rod when advancing electrode (keep in gas shield) to avoid oxidation, and hold torch on puddle until solidified when finished.

nodes.name: TIG Post-Use Shutdown Procedure
nodes.summary: Close torch gas valve, turn Right Knob OFF, set torch down safely, turn Power Switch OFF, allow welder to cool, unplug power cord, remove ground clamp, disconnect torch/ground/foot pedal cables, close and cap gas cylinder valve, disconnect gas hose, store cylinder, clean and store welder and accessories out of children's reach.

nodes.name: Strike Test
nodes.summary: Procedure performed after practice welding to check weld progress before continuing, detailed on page 34.
aliases: checking your weld with a test strike

nodes.name: Stick Grounding and Electrode Holder Handling Warning
nodes.summary: Do not weld without Grounding Clamp; when not held by operator, Electrode Holder must sit on a nonconductive, nonflammable surface.
aliases: don't set the stinger on metal warning

nodes.name: Stick Setup and Startup Procedure
nodes.summary: Steps 1-7: plug into grounded GFCI-protected 120V/240V outlet, set electrode holder down safely, power on, navigate Home/Main Knob to select Stick process, then set polarity, electrode type, electrode diameter/material thickness, and auto weld amperage settings.

nodes.name: Stick Polarity Setting
nodes.summary: Plug cables in according to screen; ground clamp is negative (-) terminal, electrode holder is positive (+) terminal; No Gas Required for Stick process.

nodes.name: Stick Electrode Type Setting
nodes.summary: Turn Main Control Knob to set electrode type; example shown 60xx/70XX.

nodes.name: Stick Electrode Diameter and Material Thickness Setting
nodes.summary: Turn Left Knob to set electrode diameter, Right Knob to set material thickness; example shown 3/32" diameter and 16 Ga thickness.

nodes.name: Stick Auto Weld Amperage Setting
nodes.summary: Turn Left Knob to adjust output amperage; turn Right Knob to ON to energize Electrode Holder, creating Open Circuit Voltage. Example display shows 60A output, Hot Start 6, Arc Force 0.

nodes.name: Stick Open Circuit Voltage Shock Hazard
nodes.summary: Once Electrode Holder is energized by turning Right Knob ON, Open Circuit Voltage is present, presenting a serious injury risk.

nodes.name: Stick Optional Settings Menu
nodes.summary: Optional settings menu accessed via Main Control Knob offering Hot Start (adjust start-of-weld amperage), Arc Force (adjust penetration/smoothness), Recall Setting, and Save Setting (up to 5 configs).

nodes.name: Stick Arc Ignition and Welding Technique
nodes.summary: Steps 8-10: place bare electrode end in Electrode Holder jaws, ignite arc by tapping/stroking/striking like a match, then lift electrode off workpiece by a distance equal to electrode diameter, tilt back 10-20 degrees, and drag to back of weld puddle to deposit material.

nodes.name: Stick Post-Use Shutdown Procedure
nodes.summary: Steps 11-14: lift electrode from workpiece, set Electrode Holder down safely, turn Power Switch OFF, allow welder to cool, unplug power cord, remove ground clamp, disconnect electrode holder and ground cables, clean and store welder and accessories out of children's reach.

nodes.name: Welder Outlet Power Requirements
nodes.summary: Welder requires a properly grounded, GFCI protected 120VAC (20 amp rated) outlet or a 240V outlet, with delayed action-type circuit breaker or fuses.

nodes.name: Strike Test Eye Protection Warning
nodes.summary: ANSI-approved safety goggles must be worn during the Strike Test procedure because striking a weld with a hammer can cause fragments to fly.

nodes.name: Weld Cleaning Eye Protection Warning
nodes.summary: WARNING to prevent serious injury: wear ANSI-approved safety goggles and protective wear when cleaning a weld, since sparks or chips may fly during cleaning.

nodes.name: Cleaning Weld Slag
nodes.summary: Procedure for removing slag from flux-cored or stick welds: use a chipping hammer to knock off slag without damaging the weld or base material, then use a wire brush or angle grinder (sold separately) to further clean or shape the weld.

nodes.name: Chipping Hammer and Wire Brush
nodes.summary: Combination hand tool with a pointed chipping hammer head on one end and a wire brush on the other end, used to clean slag and debris from welds.

nodes.name: Coat of Slag Over Weld
nodes.summary: Slag is a necessary byproduct of flux-cored wire welding (and stick welding) that shields the weld from impurities. It must be cleaned off after welding using a chipping hammer and wire brush. Gas-shielded MIG welds do not produce slag since the shielding gas protects the weld.
aliases: that crusty layer on the weld

nodes.name: Contact-Tip-to-Work Distance (CTWD)
nodes.summary: Distance from the contact tip to the workpiece during wire welding. Shorter CTWD increases heat/penetration; longer CTWD reduces heat/penetration. Recommended to maintain 1/2 inch or less for proper technique; excessive CTWD contributes to porosity, spatter, and crooked beads.
aliases: how far to hold the tip

nodes.name: Inadequate Penetration (Wire Welding)
nodes.summary: Weld does not penetrate the joint fully, staying just on the surface; indicates workpiece is 'not hot enough'. Caused by incorrect welding technique, insufficient weld heat, workpieces too thick/close, or insufficient weld material.

nodes.name: Excess Penetration or Burn-Through (Wire Welding)
nodes.summary: Weld droops on top and underneath, or falls through entirely making a hole; indicates workpiece is 'too hot'. Caused by workpiece overheating, travel speed too slow, or excessive material at weld.

nodes.name: Increase Workpiece Heat and Penetration (Wire Welding)
nodes.summary: To weld thicker workpieces properly: increase weld current, decrease travel speed, use faster wire feed, or use shorter CTWD.

nodes.name: Reduce Workpiece Heat and Limit Penetration (Wire Welding)
nodes.summary: To weld thinner workpieces properly: decrease weld current, increase travel speed, use slower wire feed, or use longer CTWD.

nodes.name: Voltage Too Low or Wire Feed Too Slow
nodes.summary: Weld bead defect: small rounded bead sitting high with shallow, uneven penetration. Corrected by increasing output voltage or increasing wire feed speed.

nodes.name: Voltage Too High or Wire Feed Too Fast
nodes.summary: Weld bead defect: wide flat bead with excessive spatter and deep uneven penetration. Corrected by decreasing output voltage or decreasing wire feed speed.

nodes.name: Travel Speed Too Fast (Wire Welding)
nodes.summary: Weld bead defect: narrow bead with tight ripple pattern and shallow penetration. Corrected by traveling slower.
aliases: went too fast down the seam

nodes.name: Travel Speed Too Slow (Wire Welding)
nodes.summary: Weld bead defect: wide bead with excessive buildup and wide penetration. Corrected by traveling faster.

nodes.name: CTWD Too Long or Wrong Polarity
nodes.summary: Weld bead defect: irregular bead with heavy spatter and inconsistent penetration. Corrected by checking polarity and maintaining less than 1/2 inch CTWD.

nodes.name: Bend at Joint
nodes.summary: Weld joint bends/deforms due to improper clamping or excessive heat. Fixed by clamping workpieces securely, making tack welds, welding small portions and letting cool, increasing travel speed, or reducing wire feed speed.

nodes.name: Weld Not Adhering Properly
nodes.summary: Gaps present between weld and previous bead or between weld and workpiece. Caused by incorrect welding technique, insufficient weld heat, dirty workpiece, insufficient weld material, or workpiece gap too narrow.

nodes.name: Porosity (Wire Welding)
nodes.summary: Small cavities or holes in the weld bead caused by incorrect polarity, insufficient/incorrect shielding gas (MIG only), dirty workpiece or wire, inconsistent travel speed, or CTWD too long.

nodes.name: Excessive Spatter (Wire Welding)
nodes.summary: Grainy, large spatter (fine spatter is normal) caused by dirty workpiece/wire, incorrect polarity, insufficient shielding gas (MIG only), wire feeding too fast, or CTWD too long.

nodes.name: Crooked/Wavy Bead
nodes.summary: Uneven, wavy bead caused by inaccurate welding technique, inconsistent travel speed, or CTWD too long. Fixed by using two hands or resting hand on steady surface, maintaining steady travel speed, and reducing CTWD.

nodes.name: Burn-Through (Wire Welding)
nodes.summary: Base material melts away leaving a hole in the weld, caused by workpiece overheating, travel speed too slow, or excessive material at weld.
aliases: blew a hole in thin metal
workpiece got too hot and holed

nodes.name: Reduce Wire Feed Speed
nodes.summary: Corrective action lowering wire feed speed to address excess penetration, excessive spatter, burn-through, or excessive material buildup.
aliases: turn down the wire speed knob

nodes.name: Increase Wire Feed Speed
nodes.summary: Corrective action raising wire feed speed to address voltage too low, insufficient weld material, or weld not adhering properly.
aliases: turn up the wire speed knob

nodes.name: Check Polarity
nodes.summary: Corrective procedure verifying polarity is set correctly for the type of welding being performed; used to resolve porosity, excessive spatter, and CTWD/polarity related defects.

nodes.name: Clean Workpiece to Bare Metal
nodes.summary: Corrective procedure of cleaning workpiece and welding wire down to bare metal, free from oil, coatings, and residues, to resolve porosity, excessive spatter, and weld adhesion problems.
aliases: clean off the rust and paint
get it down to clean metal

nodes.name: Inadequate Penetration (Stick Welding)
nodes.summary: Weld bead sits on top without fusing into base metal; indicates workpiece is 'not hot enough' during stick welding.
aliases: stick weld's just sitting on top

nodes.name: Excess Penetration or Burn-Through (Stick Welding)
nodes.summary: Penetration extends fully through workpiece creating a hole; indicates workpiece is 'too hot' during stick welding.

nodes.name: Increase Workpiece Heat and Penetration (Stick Welding)
nodes.summary: To weld thicker workpieces properly with stick welding: increase current and/or weld more slowly.
aliases: crank the current on the stick machine
more heat for thick metal stick welding
turn up the stick welder amps

nodes.name: Reduce Workpiece Heat and Limit Penetration (Stick Welding)
nodes.summary: To weld thinner workpieces properly with stick welding: decrease current and/or weld more quickly.
aliases: turn down the stick welder amps

nodes.name: Current Too Low (Stick Welding)
nodes.summary: Stick weld defect showing small, rounded, piled-up bead. Corrected by increasing the current.
aliases: amps too low on the stinger
not enough juice on stick weld

nodes.name: Current Too High (Stick Welding)
nodes.summary: Stick weld defect showing wide, flat, irregular bead with heavy undercut appearance. Corrected by decreasing the current.
aliases: amps too high on the stinger

nodes.name: Weld Speed Too Fast (Stick Welding)
nodes.summary: Stick weld defect showing narrow bead. Corrected by welding slower.
aliases: went too quick with the rod

nodes.name: Weld Speed Too Slow (Stick Welding)
nodes.summary: Stick weld defect showing wide, bulky bead. Corrected by welding faster.

nodes.name: Arc Length Too Short (Stick Welding)
nodes.summary: Stick weld defect showing small, tight bead due to electrode held too close to workpiece. Corrected by increasing distance between electrode and workpiece.

nodes.name: Arc Length Too Long (Stick Welding)
nodes.summary: Stick weld defect showing spattered, irregular bead due to electrode held too far from workpiece. Corrected by decreasing distance between electrode and workpiece.

nodes.name: Clean Stick Weld Before Inspection
nodes.summary: Stick welds are covered with a coat of slag and must be cleaned before inspecting or evaluating the weld appearance.

nodes.name: TIG Welding Complexity Notice
nodes.summary: NOTICE: TIG welding is a complicated process requiring experience and skill; training beyond the scope of this manual is required to TIG weld properly.

nodes.name: Stick Weld Excess Penetration or Burn-Through
nodes.summary: Weld droops on top and underneath or falls through entirely, making a hole, due to workpiece overheating or too-slow welding speed.
aliases: melted a hole with the rod

nodes.name: Stick Weld Proper Penetration Reference
nodes.summary: Reference condition where weld is visible underneath and bulges slightly on top, indicating correct penetration.

nodes.name: Stick Weld Inadequate Penetration
nodes.summary: Weld does not contact the joint fully, sitting only on the surface, due to incorrect technique, insufficient heat, workpiece thickness/fit-up, or insufficient fill material.
aliases: stick weld just on the surface

nodes.name: Stick Weld Not Adhering Properly
nodes.summary: Gaps present between weld and previous bead or between weld and workpiece, caused by technique, heat, cleanliness, fill material, or joint gap issues.

nodes.name: Stick Weld Bend at Joint
nodes.summary: Weld bends at the joint between two workpieces, caused by improper clamping or excessive heat.
aliases: joint bent from stick welding heat

nodes.name: Stick Weld Porosity
nodes.summary: Small cavities or holes in the weld bead caused by dirty workpiece/fill material or inconsistent welding speed.

nodes.name: Stick Weld Crooked/Wavy Bead
nodes.summary: Weld bead curves and waves irregularly instead of running straight, caused by inaccurate welding or inconsistent welding speed.

nodes.name: Stick Weld Excessive Spatter
nodes.summary: Grainy, large spatter (as opposed to normal fine spatter) caused by dirty workpiece or fill material.

nodes.name: Stick Weld Burn-Through (Top View)
nodes.summary: Base material melts away leaving a hole in the weld, caused by workpiece overheating, too-slow welding speed, or excessive fill material.
aliases: burned clean through with the rod

nodes.name: Reduce Current to Fix Overheating
nodes.summary: Reduce welding current to correct workpiece overheating causing burn-through or excess penetration.

nodes.name: Increase and Steady Welding Speed
nodes.summary: Increase welding speed and keep it steady to prevent overheating, burn-through, and inconsistent bead defects like porosity or crooked beads.
aliases: speed up and keep it steady

nodes.name: Keep Arc on Leading Edge of Weld Puddle
nodes.summary: Maintain proper arc position on the leading edge of the weld puddle and hold torch/electrode at proper angles to ensure full joint contact and adhesion.

nodes.name: Slow Down and Increase Current for Fill Melt-In
nodes.summary: Slow welding speed to allow fill material time to melt into the weld location and increase current to add sufficient heat.
aliases: give the fill rod more juice
slow down and turn up the heat

nodes.name: Bevel Thick Workpieces and Weld in Passes
nodes.summary: Bevel thick workpieces, allow a slight gap, and weld in several passes to achieve adequate penetration.
aliases: grind a bevel on thick metal
leave a gap and weld in layers

nodes.name: Increase Amount of Fill Material
nodes.summary: Add more fill material to correct insufficient weld material issues causing inadequate penetration or poor adherence.

nodes.name: Adjust Stringer Bead Placement and Angle
nodes.summary: Place stringer bead correctly in the joint and adjust workpiece position or weld angle to permit proper welding to the bottom of the piece.
aliases: aim the weld better in the joint

nodes.name: Decrease Workpiece Distance and Increase Bevel
nodes.summary: Decrease the distance between workpieces and increase bevel to fix poor weld adherence from excessive gap.
aliases: close up the gap and bevel more

nodes.name: Clamp Workpieces and Make Tack Welds
nodes.summary: Securely clamp workpieces and make tack welds to prevent bending at the joint from improper clamping.
aliases: clamp it down and tack it
tack it first so it doesn't move

nodes.name: Weld Small Portions and Allow Cooling
nodes.summary: Weld a small portion at a time and allow it to cool before proceeding, and increase weld speed, to reduce excessive heat causing joint bending.
aliases: do a little at a time
weld in short sections and let it cool

nodes.name: Clean Slag with Chipping Hammer and Wire Brush
nodes.summary: Remove the necessary protective slag coating from a stick weld after welding using a Chipping Hammer and Wire Brush.

nodes.name: Use Two Hands or Steady Hand Rest for Accurate Welding
nodes.summary: Use two hands or rest the welding hand on a steady surface to improve welding accuracy and prevent crooked/wavy beads.
aliases: rest arm on the table while welding

nodes.name: Reduce Amount of Fill Material
nodes.summary: Reduce the amount of fill material used to prevent excessive material buildup causing burn-through.
aliases: don't lay it on so thick
ease up on the wire feed

nodes.name: Chipping Hammer
nodes.summary: Tool used to remove slag coating from a stick weld bead after welding.

nodes.name: Wire Brush
nodes.summary: Tool used alongside the chipping hammer to clean slag off a stick weld bead.

nodes.name: Cool Down Before Servicing
nodes.summary: Unplug the welder, place on a heat-proof non-conductive surface, and allow all parts to cool thoroughly before servicing to prevent injury, fire, and burns.

nodes.name: Routine Maintenance Checklist
nodes.summary: Before-use inspection for loose hardware, misalignment, damaged cord/cables, cracked parts; periodic interior dust removal by a technician; and post-use dry storage.

nodes.name: MIG Gun Nozzle and Contact Tip Inspection and Cleaning
nodes.summary: Step-by-step procedure to cool and unplug the welder, remove and scrub the nozzle, inspect for damage, unscrew and clean the contact tip, verify hole shape, and reinstall or replace parts as needed.

nodes.name: Replacing the LCD Screen Cover
nodes.summary: Pry the frame off the Display using a flathead screwdriver, insert a new Screen Cover into the Screen Frame with gap facing down, and reinsert the frame top-first.

nodes.name: MIG Gun Nozzle
nodes.summary: Removable nozzle at the front of the MIG gun; should have a flat, even end and be replaced if uneven, chipped, melted, or cracked.

nodes.name: MIG Gun Contact Tip
nodes.summary: Threaded tip that guides welding wire; must have an even circular hole and correct size for wire diameter; replace if oblong, bulged, or worn.

nodes.name: LCD Screen Cover (Item 17)
nodes.summary: Protective cover for the LCD display screen, inserted into the Screen Frame with the gap facing downward.

nodes.name: LCD Screen Frame (Item 18)
nodes.summary: Frame that holds the Screen Cover and is pried off/on the Display housing using a flathead screwdriver.

nodes.name: Discharge MIG Gun Before Servicing
nodes.summary: Shut off the welder, disconnect from power, and discharge the MIG Gun to ground before adjusting, cleaning, or repairing the unit.
aliases: bleed off the gun before working on it
kill the charge in the gun

nodes.name: Discharge Electrode Before Servicing (TIG/Stick)
nodes.summary: Shut off the welder, disconnect from power, and discharge the electrode to ground before adjusting, cleaning, or repairing the unit.

nodes.name: Wire Feed Motor Runs but Wire Does Not Feed
nodes.summary: Motor runs but wire fails to feed properly, caused by insufficient feed pressure, wrong roller size, damaged gun/cable/liner, or overly tight feed tensioner.
aliases: motor spins but no wire comes out
no wire even though motor's turning

nodes.name: Wire Bird's Nesting
nodes.summary: Wire tangles into a bird's nest during operation due to excess feed pressure, wrong contact tip size, loose gun cable connector, or damaged liner.

nodes.name: Wire Stops During Welding
nodes.summary: Wire feed halts mid-weld due to bent gun cable, clogged/worn/undersized liner, tangled spool, or feed roller contact issues.

nodes.name: Unstable Welding Arc (MIG/Flux-Cored)
nodes.summary: Arc instability from improper wire feed, worn contact tip/liner, incorrect wire feed speed, loose cables, incorrect polarity, poor gas coverage, or poor workpiece connection.

nodes.name: Weak Arc Strength (MIG/Flux-Cored)
nodes.summary: Weak arc caused by incorrect line voltage, improper cord gauge/length, or insufficient current setting.

nodes.name: Weak Arc Strength (TIG/Stick)
nodes.summary: Weak arc from incorrect line voltage or improper cord gauge/length on TIG/Stick processes.
aliases: not enough power for stick welding

nodes.name: Unstable Welding Arc (TIG/Stick)
nodes.summary: Arc instability from loose electrode/ground cable, damaged electrode holder, incorrect current setting, or low shielding gas.

nodes.name: Welder Does Not Function When Switched On (MIG/Flux-Cored)
nodes.summary: Welder fails to operate due to tripped thermal protection, insufficient input voltage/amperage, faulty trigger, voltage protection lockout, or incorrect mode selection.

nodes.name: LCD Display Does Not Light (MIG/Flux-Cored)
nodes.summary: Display fails to light due to improper outlet connection, unpowered outlet, wrong plug rating, tripped breaker, or loose input power cord.

nodes.name: Wire Feeds but Arc Does Not Ignite
nodes.summary: Wire feeds normally but arc fails to start due to improper ground connection or improperly sized/worn/dirty contact tip.
aliases: no arc even though wire's feeding
wire comes out but no arc

nodes.name: Porosity in Weld Metal
nodes.summary: Porosity caused by empty gas bottle, incorrect gas flow, dirty workpiece, excessive CTWD, incorrect polarity, or dirty welding wire.

nodes.name: Welder Does Not Function When Switched On (TIG/Stick)
nodes.summary: Welder fails to operate due to tripped thermal protection, faulty trigger, unattached ground clamp, or disconnected shielding gas.

nodes.name: LCD Display Does Not Light (TIG/Stick)
nodes.summary: Display fails to light due to improper outlet connection or unpowered outlet/circuit breaker/GFCI trip.

nodes.name: Increase Wire Feed Pressure
nodes.summary: Adjust wire feed pressure per step 27 (page 17) to resolve insufficient feed or excess feed causing bird's nesting.

nodes.name: Adjust Feed Tensioner
nodes.summary: Loosen or set the Feed Tensioner so it applies only enough pressure to stop spinning after trigger release, preventing bird's nesting and feed issues.

nodes.name: Select Correct Feed Roller Size
nodes.summary: Flip or replace the feed roller to the correct groove size per Feed Roller instructions on page 12.

nodes.name: Properly Insert MIG Gun Cable Connector
nodes.summary: Insert Gun Cable Connector fully into Wire Feed mechanism with no O-Rings exposed, per steps 13-14 page 13, to ensure gas coverage and prevent bird's nesting.
aliases: hook up the MIG gun cable properly
make sure the gun plug's fully in
plug the gun cable in right

nodes.name: Polarity DCEP for MIG Welding
nodes.summary: MIG welding requires DCEP (direct current electrode positive) polarity for a stable arc and proper weld quality.

nodes.name: Polarity DCEN for Flux-Cored Self-Shielded Welding
nodes.summary: Flux-Cored self-shielded welding requires DCEN (direct current electrode negative) polarity, differing from MIG's DCEP.

nodes.name: Gas Flow Rate per Settings Chart
nodes.summary: Shielding gas flow rate should be set according to the Settings Chart to maintain stable arc and prevent porosity.
aliases: set gas flow per the chart

nodes.name: Check Ground Clamp Connection
nodes.summary: Verify ground clamp connection to workpiece and machine, and ensure MIG Gun is properly secured, to resolve unstable arc.

nodes.name: Switch Current to Proper Setting for Metal Thickness
nodes.summary: Adjust current setting appropriately for the metal thickness being welded to resolve weak arc strength.

nodes.name: Do Not Use Extension Cord
nodes.summary: Do not use an extension cord on this welder; use only the supplied power cords or an identical replacement cord.

nodes.name: Duty Cycle Reference (MIG/Flux-Cored)
nodes.summary: Reference to Duty Cycle (Duration of Use) information on page 19 for reducing welding duration/frequency to prevent thermal shutdown.
aliases: how long you can weld before resting (MIG)

nodes.name: Duty Cycle Reference (TIG/Stick)
nodes.summary: Reference to Duty Cycle (Duration of Use) information on page 29 for reducing welding duration/frequency to prevent thermal shutdown.
aliases: how long you can weld before resting (stick)

nodes.name: Reset Button
nodes.summary: Button on back of machine to reset circuit breaker or clear low/over-voltage protection after verifying correct input voltage.
aliases: the little reset button on back

nodes.name: Check Contact Tip to Work Distance (CTWD)
nodes.summary: Verify proper contact-tip-to-workpiece distance to prevent porosity when gun is used too far from workpiece.

nodes.name: Clean Workpiece to Bare Metal
nodes.summary: Clean workpiece surface down to bare metal to eliminate contamination causing porosity.
aliases: get rid of rust/paint before welding

nodes.name: Replace Shielding Gas Cylinder
nodes.summary: Replace shielding gas cylinder when supply is low, to resolve unstable arc in TIG/Stick welding.

nodes.name: Attach Ground Clamp to Workpiece
nodes.summary: Ensure the Ground Clamp is attached to the workpiece to allow welder to function (TIG/Stick).

nodes.name: MCU Board
nodes.summary: Main control board connecting via CN5/CN7 34-pin ribbon connectors to gate-driver stages controlling IGBT switches; interfaces with LCD screen and remote board.

nodes.name: Remote Board
nodes.summary: Board connected to MCU via CN8 (7-pin), providing two aviation plug connections for remote accessories.

nodes.name: Transformer T1 (T60*30*28)
nodes.summary: Main power transformer with secondary taps feeding the rectifier bridge and IGBT/PFC module.

nodes.name: Output Hall Sensor
nodes.summary: Hall effect sensor installed on the OUT+ output line, wired through connector CN1, used for output current sensing.

nodes.name: Wire Feeder Motor (M)
nodes.summary: Motor driving the wire feed mechanism, connected via CN3 (4-pin) and CN4 (5-pin) connectors to the control system.
aliases: the motor that pushes the wire

nodes.name: Ordering Replacement Parts
nodes.summary: Procedure for ordering replacement parts by recording the product's date code (or purchase date) and specifying UPC 193175422590; notes some parts are illustration-only and not individually available, and repairs should be performed by certified/licensed technicians only.

nodes.name: Buyer Repair Liability Disclaimer
nodes.summary: Manufacturer/distributor disclaims warranty regarding buyer's qualification to perform repairs or replace parts; states repairs should be undertaken by certified and licensed technicians, and buyer assumes all risk for self-repairs or installations.

nodes.name: Install 2 lb Wire Spool
nodes.summary: Install a 2 lb wire spool using wingnut, spacer, spindle, and brake pad; insert wire through both feed guides and clamp into feed mechanism. An adapter and knob are required for 10-12 lb spools.

nodes.name: Wire Spool Spindle
nodes.summary: Spindle that holds the wire spool in place.

nodes.name: Wire Spool Brake Pad
nodes.summary: Brake pad providing tension/friction for the wire spool.

nodes.name: 10-12 lb Spool Adapter and Knob
nodes.summary: Adapter and knob required to load a 10-12 lb wire spool instead of the standard 2 lb spool setup.

nodes.name: 2 Inch Contact Tip
nodes.summary: Contact tip consumable removed prior to purging gun of air and wire feeding.

nodes.name: Purge Gun and Feed Wire
nodes.summary: Remove nozzle and tip, plug selected cord into welder and wall, turn welder on, and press cold wire feed switch until wire comes out the front of the gun.

nodes.name: Wire Feed Tension Knob
nodes.summary: Knob incrementally tightened clockwise to set wire feed tension; correctly tensioned wire will bend at 2"-3" against a wood block.

data.test: wire bends at 2-3 inches against wood block

nodes.name: Unplug and Replace Nozzle and Tip
nodes.summary: Unplug welder and replace nozzle and contact tip after tensioning wire feed.

nodes.name: Hold Gun Clear and Set Weld Parameters
nodes.summary: Hold gun clear of workpiece, then plug in welder, turn it on, and set weld thickness and other settings before welding.

nodes.name: Read Manual Before Use Warning
nodes.summary: To prevent serious injury, read all manual warnings and instructions before using the welder.

nodes.name: Keep Torch and Wire Clear of Grounded Objects Warning
nodes.summary: To prevent fire and serious injury, keep torch and wire clear of grounded objects while the welder is plugged in.
aliases: keep gun away from ground warning

nodes.name: Stick Cable Setup
nodes.summary: Connect ground clamp to negative terminal, electrode holder to positive terminal, and leave wire feed power disconnected for stick welding.

nodes.name: MIG Cable Setup
nodes.summary: Connect ground clamp to negative terminal, wire feed power to positive terminal, MIG gun to wire feed, and wire feed control inside welder.

nodes.name: Flux-Cored Cable Setup
nodes.summary: Follows MIG cable setup but with reversed polarity: ground clamp to positive terminal, wire feed power to negative terminal.

nodes.name: TIG Cable Setup
nodes.summary: Connect ground clamp to positive terminal, TIG torch to negative terminal, gas line to gas regulator, foot pedal inside welder, and leave wire feed power disconnected.

nodes.name: Negative Terminal
nodes.summary: Rear panel terminal used for ground clamp (stick/MIG) or wire feed power (flux-cored) or TIG torch connection, depending on process.

nodes.name: Positive Terminal
nodes.summary: Rear panel terminal used for electrode holder (stick), wire feed power (MIG), ground clamp (flux-cored/TIG), depending on process.

nodes.name: Gas Regulator Connection
nodes.summary: Connection point on welder for gas line used during TIG welding.
aliases: where the gas line screws in

nodes.name: Foot Pedal Connection (Internal)
nodes.summary: Internal welder connection point for TIG foot pedal.

nodes.name: Flux-Cored Typical Applications
nodes.summary: Typical applications for FCAW: galvanized steel, general fabrication, pipe and tubing, maintenance & repair.

nodes.name: MIG Typical Applications
nodes.summary: Typical applications for MIG: sheet metal, automotive body, tubing, structural steel.

nodes.name: Stick Typical Applications
nodes.summary: Typical applications for Stick: pipe and tubing, structural steel, pressure vessels, maintenance & repair.

nodes.name: TIG Typical Applications
nodes.summary: Typical applications for TIG: stainless steel exhausts, bicycle frames, thin wall pipe & tubing, metal art.

nodes.name: MIG vs Flux-Cored Comparison
nodes.summary: Side-by-side comparison of MIG and Flux-Cored characteristics: wire type, shielding gas use, spatter, surface prep, outdoor use, penetration, and suitability for thin materials.

nodes.name: Duty Cycle Definition
nodes.summary: Duty cycle is the number of minutes, within a 10-minute period, a welding process can produce the specified welding current without overheating. Example: 165A @ 30% Duty Cycle = 3 minutes welding, 7 minutes resting.
aliases: how long you can weld before resting

nodes.name: Welder Overheating from Exceeding Duty Cycle
nodes.summary: Exceeding the rated duty cycle (welding beyond the specified minutes within a 10-minute period at a given current) causes the welder to overheat.

nodes.name: Respect Duty Cycle Rest Period
nodes.summary: To avoid overheating, rest the welder for the remaining minutes within the 10-minute period after reaching the duty-cycle-rated welding time at a given amperage (e.g., 165A @ 30% = weld 3 min, rest 7 min).

nodes.name: Identify Input Voltage Before Selecting Welder
nodes.summary: Important: users must identify their input voltage (120 Volt or 240 Volt) before selecting a welding process/machine, since capabilities and settings differ by voltage.

nodes.name: Weld Bead Cleanliness Comparison Photos
nodes.summary: Four photos comparing weld bead spatter/cleanliness for Flux-Cored, MIG, Stick, and TIG processes under the 'How Clean Do You Need Your Weld?' question.

nodes.name: Timed flux-cored setup from scratch
nodes.summary: Starting with a new spool not installed and all leads disconnected, the presenter times himself setting up the OmniPro 220 for flux-cored welding, taking 4.5 minutes total including wire loading and lead connections.
aliases: setting up gasless wire from scratch

nodes.name: Flux-cored test welds on plate and coupons
nodes.summary: A flat horizontal weld on 3/16 inch plate and a fillet weld on 1/8 inch coupons are performed using .035 Vulcan flux-cored wire with the synergic auto settings, both turning out well.
aliases: trying out flux wire on plate

nodes.name: Switching feed roller and polarity for MIG
nodes.summary: The presenter demonstrates switching the feed roller from knurled to V-groove, connecting shielding gas (100% CO2), and swapping the leads to DCEP polarity for solid core MIG wire setup, noting it took about 5.5 minutes total.
aliases: changing rollers and wiring for MIG
swapping the drive roller for MIG

nodes.name: MIG synergic settings evaluation
nodes.summary: The presenter confirms that the synergetic/auto weld settings produced good results for the MIG welds using .035 solid core Vulcan wire.

nodes.name: Quick stick welding setup
nodes.summary: Stick welding setup is shown to be the fastest, requiring only connecting the ground clamp and electrode holder in about 30 seconds, with the machine prompting for 60 or 70 series rod selection.

nodes.name: Stick welds on plate and t-joint coupon
nodes.summary: Using 7018 1/8 inch rod, a weld is demonstrated on 3/16 inch flat plate followed by a fillet weld on a t-joint using eighth inch coupons.
aliases: stick welding on plate and T-joint

nodes.name: TIG setup with foot pedal and lift-arc start
nodes.summary: The presenter sets up TIG welding with a foot pedal for amperage control, explaining that without high-frequency start, the lift-arc method is used: touching tungsten to the workpiece, stepping the pedal, then lifting to strike the arc; total setup including torch, tungsten grinding and gas hookup took about 8 minutes.
aliases: setting up TIG with the pedal

nodes.name: TIG welds on coupons evaluation
nodes.summary: TIG test welds performed on 1/8 inch coupons show the machine's preset auto settings work reasonably well, placing the OmniPro's TIG performance in the middle of the spectrum compared to other machines.
